front: Work: 69 black-and-white self-portraits posing as unnamed women in stereotypical roles evocative of 1950s–60s B-movies, including 'The Hitchhiker' and 'City Girl'
Artist: Pictures Generation photographer who uses herself as the model in all her photographs; Chuck Close called her 'perhaps the most important artist of the 1980s'
Photograph: Untitled #96 shows this artist lying on a kitchen floor in orange, clutching a crumpled newspaper ad; was briefly the second most expensive photograph ever sold
Work: uses medical dolls, prosthetic limbs, and mannequin parts in sexually explicit arrangements to critique pornography; created during the debates over NEA funding of Mapplethorpe and Serrano
Work: self-portraits imitating Old Master paintings; includes reworkings of Raphael's Fornarina and Caravaggio's Young Sick Bacchus (with fake grapes)
Work: color photographs subverting the magazine centerfold format — showing women in distressed or pensive (rather than seductive) poses; commissioned by Artforum
Artist: Laura Mulvey's 1991 essay 'A Phantasmagoria of the Female Body' analyzed this photographer's work on stereotypical female imagery
Artist: had a brief relationship with Robert Longo, who included her in his Men in the Cities series
Work: self-portraits wearing clown outfits in front of digitally manipulated backgrounds; multiple clown figures superimposed on each other
